nativeRange.habitat: Shallow bogs, stream margins, and moist terrestrial soils across temperate East Asia and the Asia-Pacific; grows readily emersed and adapts to fully submerged aquarium culture

decorPreferences: open foreground area for a creeping carpet
nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or fine gravel with root tabs)
hardscape (driftwood, rock) for climbing or trailing growth

goodWith: small bottom-dwellers that benefit from carpet cover
avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large cichlids
burrowing fish that uproot foreground carpets

summary: Japanese Dwarf Pennywort (Hydrocotyle tripartita 'Japan') is a fast-growing foreground plant with small, clover-shaped bright green leaves on creeping stems. Under strong light with CO2 it forms a dense low carpet; under lower light it stretches upward and can serve as a midground accent.
careNotes: Japanese Dwarf Pennywort is a well-established cultivar of Hydrocotyle tripartita that has been widely used in planted aquascaping since being popularized in the early 2000s. It is a distinct species from other Hydrocotyle plants sometimes encountered in the trade, including the whorled-leaf H. verticillata, the smaller-leaved H. sibthorpioides, and the larger, coin-leaf trailing H. leucocephala — each has a different growth habit and care profile. Temperature is generally suited to 20–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, and soft-to-moderately-hard water. Light demand is generally rated medium to medium-high; under bright light the plant grows compact and low, forming the dense green carpet the species is known for, while under lower light it tends to stretch upward and trail rather than carpet. A fine-grained, nutrient-rich substrate (such as aquasoil) is broadly recommended, and regular liquid fertilization — particularly iron and potassium — or root tabs help maintain vibrant green coloration. Under optimal carpet conditions adult height is typically 3–7 cm; untrained stems can reach 10 cm or more before flopping. The plant is fast-growing and benefits from regular trimming; without it, new growth will quickly overshadow smaller neighbouring foreground species. Although the plant can survive in lower-tech setups, reliable carpet formation generally requires CO2 supplementation and higher light levels, which is why care is rated intermediate rather than easy.
breedingNotes: Japanese Dwarf Pennywort is propagated vegetatively by cuttings and runners. The creeping stem can be cut into segments and replanted roughly 1.5 inches apart to encourage carpet formation, or dense clumps can be pulled apart and the rooted sections replanted separately. Trimming new shoots along the stem tends to promote thicker, more compact growth rather than upward stretching. Tissue-culture stock is available from a number of specialty online retailers and arrives free of algae and snails — a practical choice when starting a new tank.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Official cultivar source. Used for category (stem plant, carpet-forming), height (5-10 cm), light demand (medium, 0.5 W/L), CO2 demand (low), care difficulty (medium), placement (foreground/carpet), growth rate (fast), and origin (Asia). The originally supplied URL https://tropica.com/en/plants/plantdetails/Hydrocotyletripartita(105D)/4671 returned an XSLT parse error; this canonical product-id URL was used instead.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (6.5-7.5), light demand (medium), CO2 (required), growth rate (moderate-fast), placement (foreground/midground/background versatile), height (5-10 inches), care difficulty (easy-moderate), and propagation (cuttings, splitting clumps). The originally supplied URL https://buceplant.com/products/hydrocotyle-tripartita-japan returned 404; the species-level Buce Plant product page is the same listing. Buce lists family as 'Apiaceae' (older taxonomy) — rejected in favour of Araliaceae per modern APG taxonomy and consistency with other Hydrocotyle entries.
